## Severity: High

## Problem

Gold-standard briefs affirmatively address the strongest arguments *against* the position. The whitepaper currently does not seriously engage with counterarguments.

## Affected Files

- `docs/legal/legal--skill-based-contest-whitepaper.md`

## Required Work

Add a new section (between current §9 and §10) titled **"Adverse Authority and Counterarguments"** covering:

1. **Pool-prize argument:** Forfeiture redistribution creates a "pool prize" potentially indistinguishable from gambling pot mechanics
2. **Metabolic variance as chance:** Weight fluctuation from water retention, hormonal cycles, illness could be characterized as a chance element under the material element test
3. **Consideration not eliminated:** The deposit-contract theory hasn't been tested — a court could hold that depositing funds into an escrow that may be forfeited IS gambling consideration
4. **State AG enforcement discretion:** AGs in consumer-protection-aggressive states (NY, CA, IL) could challenge the classification regardless of legal merit
5. **"House always wins" optics:** Platform fee structure means Styx profits regardless of user outcomes — regulators may view this as a house edge

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] New section ≥500 words
- [ ] Each counterargument has a rebuttal with supporting authority
- [ ] Uses *But see* and *Cf.* Bluebook signals appropriately
- [ ] Includes at least 3 adverse cases or AG opinions

## Severity: Medium

## Problem

Consent matrix flags heart rate data as "MAY qualify" as biometric under BIPA (740 Ill. Comp. Stat. 14/15) without analyzing the statutory definition or controlling case law.

## Affected Files

- `docs/legal/legal--cross-jurisdictional-consent-matrix.md`

## Required Analysis

- BIPA § 10 definition of "biometric identifier" — does passively collected wearable heart rate data meet the threshold?
- *Rosenbach v. Six Flags Entertainment Corp.*, 2019 IL 123186 (standing under BIPA)
- *Cothron v. White Castle System, Inc.*, 2023 IL 128004 (per-scan accrual of BIPA claims)
- *Rivera v. Google LLC* (N.D. Ill. 2020) (what constitutes a "biometric identifier")
- Texas CUBI (Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 503.001) — parallel analysis
- Washington My Health My Data Act — scope determination for wearable health data

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] "MAY qualify" replaced with definitive analysis + recommendation
- [ ] Illinois consent template updated if heart rate IS biometric
- [ ] Texas CUBI consent template added if needed
- [ ] ≥3 BIPA cases cited with Bluebook format

comments.body:
Completed Lane D — Biometric/privacy sprint for Issue #566.

### Key Updates:
1. **Definitive Classification:** Heart rate data is confirmed NOT to be a "biometric identifier" under Illinois BIPA (740 ILCS 14/10) or Texas CUBI (Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 503.001) due to the narrow, list-limited statutory definitions.
2. **Washington MHMDA Scope:** Heart rate data IS classified as "consumer health data" and "biometric data" under Washington's My Health My Data Act (Wash. Rev. Code § 19.373.005), requiring explicit opt-in and granting consumers an absolute right of deletion.
3. **Texas TDPSA Alignment:** Wearable heart rate data is regulated as "sensitive data" under the Texas Data Privacy and Security Act (TDPSA).
4. **Consent Matrix Update:** Refreshed `docs/legal/legal--cross-jurisdictional-consent-matrix.md` with updated jurisdiction logic and Bluebook citations for *Rosenbach*, *Cothron*, and *Rivera*.
5. **New Templates:** Added jurisdictional consent templates for Texas (TDPSA) and Washington (MHMDA).

All acceptance criteria met.

body:
## Problem

`.env.example` now declares 8 brand bindings as the canonical authority for project identity (commit `4239476`, Project Identity section):

- `CANONICAL_DOMAIN`, `PROJECT_NAME`, `MOBILE_BUNDLE_ID`, `DEEP_LINK_SCHEME`
- `CONTACT_EMAIL_DOMAIN`, `RENDER_API_SERVICE_NAME`, `RENDER_WEB_SERVICE_NAME`, `CANONICAL_REPO_URL`

The same values are also duplicated by hand across:

- `apps/mobile/ios/Info.plist`
- `apps/mobile/android/build.gradle`
- `render.yaml`
- `package.json` files
- `docker-compose.yml`
- ngrok config yaml
- `legal/*.md`, `outreach/*.md`, `docs/architecture/*.md`

Each duplication is a drift risk. Propagation discipline is currently convention-only (a NOTE block in `.env.example` documents which files to update in lockstep).

## Closure options

- **(a)** Pre-commit hook diffs `.env.example` brand values against propagation targets, fails on mismatch (~60 min including tests)
- **(b)** Generator script materializes platform-specific files from `.env` at build time via \`envsubst\` or \`mustache\` (~90 min, more invasive but durable)
- **(c)** Audit-only — document the propagation map in \`docs/architecture/brand-propagation-map.md\`, no enforcement (~30 min)

## Acceptance

Changing a brand value in `.env.example` either (1) propagates automatically to all targets at build time, or (2) fails CI loudly with a list of out-of-sync files.

## Context

Discovered during a root-layer pass on the canonical domain stand-up plan (\`docs/planning/planning--styx-sequence-b-a-c--canonical-domain-standup--2026-05-17.md\`). Pre-blocker to plan Phase 2 (propagation hardening).

Internal cross-reference: IRF-III-049.
title: Propagate brand identity from .env to platform-specific files at build time

body:
## Problem

Recent planning docs in \`~/.claude/plans/\` use generic letter labels (\`A\`, \`B\`, \`C\`) for plan branches when descriptive names would carry more meaning and survive context loss better.

Example from the canonical domain stand-up plan:
- \`A\` = market activation
- \`B\` = IRF propagation
- \`C\` = audit-engine extraction

Letter labels require the reader to look up the mapping every time; descriptive names are self-documenting.

## Closure options

- **(a)** Audit \`~/.claude/plans/\` for A/B/C-labeled plans, propose descriptive renames, archive originals (~45 min)
- **(b)** Add CLAUDE.md guidance under "Plan File Discipline" prohibiting generic letter labels going forward (~10 min, documentation-only)
- **(c)** Leave existing plans, enforce naming discipline for new plans (~5 min)

## Acceptance

New plans use descriptive branch names; existing A/B/C-labeled plans either renamed or grandfathered with a deprecation note.

## Notes

The v4 of the canonical domain stand-up plan already uses descriptive sequencing alongside the A/B/C labels — every letter is mapped to a phase name in the plan body. Treat this issue as making that pattern the rule, not the exception.

Internal cross-reference: IRF-III-051.

title: Replace A/B/C plan branch labels with descriptive names

body:
Currently, all 1,831 tests across the project are unit-level tests utilizing a mocked `Pool` for database interactions. 

The Triadic Review flagged this as a real gap. Without actual database integration tests, we risk regressions and false positives, as we just saw with the loss of `EXCLUDED.*` fields during ON CONFLICT operations on submitAttestation (PR #659).

We need an integration test suite that:
1. Provisions an ephemeral real PostgreSQL instance (via testcontainers or similar)
2. Runs the database migrations
3. Tests database-coupled services with actual queries rather than mocks

This must be addressed before re-running the Triadic Review.
